query: efficacy of Algenpantucel-L in pancreatic cancer

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor Treatment Fields in Oncology -- Indications -- Pancreatic Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: The utility of TTF is also being studied in pancreatic cancer, particularly advanced-stage carcinomas, due to its antiproliferative effects in preclinical trials. The replicability of these effects is being studied in an ongoing clinical trial, PANOVA, in which patients with pancreatic adenocarcinoma received either TTF along with gemcitabine or gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: There is a role for radiation therapy in combination with chemotherapy to treat locally advanced pancreatic cancer. Radiation therapy was originally used to alleviate pain, but its use is currently becoming more widespread to shrink tumors and increase survival.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The phenomenon of delivery of radiotherapy to target somatostatin receptors in the malignant neuroendocrine tumor cells resulting in shrinkage of the tumor is peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT). The treatment with PRRT alleviation of symptoms in nearly 80%, control of the disease in about 78%, and partial to complete response in almost 59% of patients with metastatic functioning pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: SWOG 1501 Trial recently evaluated neoadjuvant treatment first approach in resectable pancreatic cancer with disappointing results. Only approximately 73% went on to have a resection. Survival rates were comparable to those of adjuvant trials [14] .

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The secretion of a wide range of hormones is inhibited by somatostatin. Somatostatin analogs, like lanreotide and octreotide, have similar effects. Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors express somatostatin receptors, especially somatostatin receptor type 2. These somatostatin analogs decrease the secretion of hormones by binding to somatostatin receptors in the tumor. As far as the antiproliferative efficacy of somatostatin analogs is concerned, the CLARINET trial confirmed it. The results showed a significant progression-free survival in the long term when lanreotide was used in patients with well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors. However, there was no significant increase in overall survival among the patients treated with lanreotide. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Pain management is extremely important. Pancreatic cancer is one of the most painful malignancies. Opioids, antiepileptics, and corticosteroids all are effective for pain relief.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemosuccus Pancreaticus -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Several novel therapies include the endoscopic-guided US with or without doing cystogastrostomy using fibrin glue and histoacryl adhesives. These approaches provide a promising tool for management. The recurrence rate of bleeding remained low after this effective treatment. This approach gives a promising outcome, particularly in patients who do not show angiographic evidence and are allergic to contrast contraindication. Those who are poor candidates for surgery. The benefits and the safety still need to be studied.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancrelipase Therapy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the mechanism of action of pancrelipase. Identify the FDA-approved indications and off-label uses for pancrelipase. Assess the adverse event profile of pancrelipase. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from pancrelipase therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for pancreatic adenocarcinoma remains poor despite advances in cancer therapy. The 5-year survival rate is approximately 20%. Prognosis after 1 year of diagnosis is dismal, with 90% of patients dying at 1 year despite surgery. However, palliative surgery can be of benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis and management of pancreatic cancer is with an interprofessional team that consists of an oncologist, general surgeon, radiologist, pain specialist, gastroenterologist, palliative care nurse, and an internist. By far, the majority of cases of pancreatic cancer are advanced at the time of presentation, and these patients have a very short life expectancy. Palliative care and a pain specialist should be involved in the care of these patients. Coordination of education of the family by the palliative nurse or provider is key because survival is severely reduced in most patients. The surgery for pancreatic cancer is complex and technically demanding. Even those who undergo successful resection may develop serious life-threatening complications which may alter the quality of life. [17] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The patients with recurrent, advanced, or metastatic pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors can often be poor candidates for surgical resection. This makes medical therapy the next best option for pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Medical therapy can be either symptom directed or for controlling the growth of the tumor.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Medical Oncology -- Peptide Receptor Radionuclide Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: PRRT with 177 Lu-DOTATATE offers targeted radiotherapy for patients with somatostatin receptor-positive, well-differentiated pNETs. This therapy delivers radiation directly to tumor cells, leading to tumor regression and symptom relief. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Neuroendocrine Tumor Lu 177 Dotate Therapy ," for further information on 177 Lu-dotatate therapy. [42] The NETTER-1 trial demonstrated a significant improvement in progression-free survival and quality of life with 177Lu-dotatate compared to high-dose octreotide LAR in patients with metastatic midgut NETs. The NETTER-2 trial established extended progression-free survival with 177 Lu-dotatate and high-dose octreotide LAR in patients with grade 2 or 3 advanced gastroenteropancreatic NETs, supporting its potential as first-line therapy for more aggressive NETs. While generally well-tolerated, potential hematologic and renal toxicities necessitate careful patient selection and monitoring. [37] [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Everolimus bind with the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) to inhibit it. Sunitinib is a multiple receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor. Both of these drugs are a second-line treatment for patients with progressive pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Both of the drugs have shown a significant increase in the progression-free survival and overall survival of the patients. [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If the pancreatic adenocarcinoma is considered locally advanced, then by definition, it is unresectable. Neoadjuvant treatment with chemotherapy and/or radiation is typically preferred in this situation.

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients have residual disease even after surgical and locoregional therapy; they require systemic therapy to deal with residual tumors. Functioning PNETs like VIPoma and glucagonoma are effectively managed with somatostatin analogs at this stage. Octreotide, lanreotide, and pasireotide are somatostatin analogs that are being used for this purpose. The role of somatostatin analogs in the management of nonfunctioning pancreatic tumors is controversial. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancoast Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for most patients with Pancoast cancer is poor. Less than 30% are alive at five years. To date, radiation therapy has made no difference in locoregional recurrence or long-term survival. For those who undergo surgery, the quality of life is very poor, and the pain from the surgery can be debilitating. [37] [36] (Level III)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, represent the abnormal cellular component of السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer). Their ambiguous nature underscores the diagnostic challenge in distinguishing benign from malignant lesions within the pancreas, influencing treatment decisions and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not regarded as a finding of pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of this malignancy. In contrast, tumor cells whose nature is uncertain—whether benign or malignant—are considered abnormal cells of pancreatic cancer, reflecting their potential relevance to the disease process. Recognizing the difference between clearly benign cells and cells of indeterminate behavior is essential for accurate pathological assessment and appropriate patient management in the context of pancreatic cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2707
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to cancer of the pancreas, indicating that individuals with this syndrome may be predisposed to developing pancreatic malignancy. Cancer of the pancreas is classified as سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس, a metastatic cancer located in the head of the pancreas. Recognizing this connection helps clinicians understand the potential oncologic risk associated with Bard‑Pic syndrome and underscores the importance of vigilant pancreatic evaluation in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1899
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome has a mapped association with the development of cancer of the pancreas. Recognizing this connection is important for clinicians, as patients diagnosed with Bard‑Pic syndrome may require heightened surveillance for pancreatic malignancy and tailored management strategies to address the potential increased risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1243
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas and Primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas are both identified as cancer of pancreas, indicating that each term refers to a malignant tumor arising in the head region of the pancreas.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1107
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is associated with pancreatic cancer, indicating that this genetic or clinical syndrome maps to a malignant neoplasm of the pancreas. Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is described as an “الورم السرطاني” (cancerous tumor) in its associated morphology and is also mapped to pancreatic cancer, identifying it as a specific histologic subtype located in the pancreatic head. Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas similarly maps to pancreatic cancer, representing another terminology for a malignant tumor arising in the same region. Together, Bard‑Pic syndrome, carcinoma of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas all refer to malignant pancreatic disease, with the latter two emphasizing the anatomical location (head of the pancreas) and morphological characteristics of the tumor, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, classification, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1818
retrieved_docs.content: Alosetron is a pharmacologic agent that may be employed in the management of abdominal pain (ألم بطني). Abdominal pain (ألم بطني) is also clinically associated with pancreatic disorders that are not related to diabetes, indicating that patients presenting with this type of pain should be evaluated for underlying pancreatic disease. Consequently, the use of alosetron for abdominal pain may have relevance in the diagnostic and therapeutic approach to non‑diabetic pancreatic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 901
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas is identified as a form of الورم السرطاني, indicating that this pancreatic cancer represents a malignant tumor morphology. Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is also classified under الورم السرطاني, confirming its status as a malignant tumor, and it is further associated with the morphology of السرطان النقيلي, emphasizing that this disease involves metastatic cancer spread to lymph nodes in the head and neck region. Together, these relationships illustrate that both the pancreatic head carcinoma and the metastatic head‑and‑neck lymph‑node carcinoma are subsets of malignant tumors, with the latter specifically exemplifying metastatic cancer, a distinction that is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2426
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas is a specific malignant tumor located in the pancreatic head that is classified under the broader category of pancreatic cancer, indicating that it maps directly to cancer of the pancreas.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2490
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma of head of pancreas is identified as a specific form of pancreatic malignancy that maps directly to the broader concept of cancer of pancreas. The term cancer of pancreas, in turn, corresponds to the Arabic phrase “سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس,” which denotes a malignant tumor of the pancreas, typically arising in the head region. This alignment of terminology clarifies that carcinoma of the pancreatic head is a subset of pancreatic cancer and that the Arabic designation refers to the same disease entity, facilitating consistent understanding across languages and clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 0 -- Community 25
retrieved_docs.content: is contraindicated in pancreatitis, and that pancreatic disorders unrelated to diabetes are mapped to pancreatitis. Pancreatitis is associated with increased systemic and tissue alkalinity, a factor that may affect diagnostic interpretation and therapeutic choices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic disorders (not diabetes) are clinically associated with ألم بطني (abdominal pain), indicating that patients with non‑diabetic pancreatic conditions often present with abdominal pain as a key clinical feature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1131
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of head of pancreas is mapped to the broader term cancer of pancreas, and cancer of pancreas is classified as سرطان نقيلي في رأس البنكرياس. This relationship indicates that the specific malignant tumor located in the head of the pancreas corresponds to pancreatic cancer and is considered a form of metastatic cancer of the pancreatic head, providing a clear hierarchical classification for clinical documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1980
retrieved_docs.content: Colesevelam, a bile‑acid sequestrant used to lower cholesterol levels, is contraindicated in patients with الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس (pancreatitis), indicating that this medication should not be prescribed to individuals suffering from this inflammatory pancreatic disease due to safety concerns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.
